The Valeriano porphyry Cu-Au deposit at the northern end of the El Indio metallogenic belt of Chile is hosted in Miocene-age granodiorite intrusions, below an outcropping lithocap with advanced argillic alteration. At the base of the lithocap a zone of nodular pyrophyllite alteration is extensive over a 2 × 1 km NW elongate zone. The nodular replacement occurs 600–1000 m above the porphyry intrusions, and ~ 0–500 m above the 0.3% Cu grade shell. The nodules consist of pyrophyllite, diaspore, dickite/kaolinite and locally alunite with pyrite that replace quartz and muscovite. Pyrophyllite (Si: Al ratio of 2:1) replacement of muscovite (1:1 ratio) during cooling requires silica, which causes quartz dissolution, as evidenced by cuspate and embayment replacement textures of quartz. Sulfur isotopic compositions of pyrite-alunite pairs (n = 18) from the nodules yield equilibrium temperatures of 375 –275 °C, consistent with established muscovite-pyrophyllite coexistence at ~ 350 –280 °C at quartz saturation. The zone of nodular alteration represents a paleo-isotherm over the causative intrusions and can be used as a vector for exploration of concealed porphyry Cu deposits elsewhere.

item.title: Similar microstructures, different interpretations: the possible anatectic record of granulite-facies marbles
item.dc:creator: Andrea Maroni
Fabrizio Tursi
Chiara Groppo
Eleanor C R Green
Richard Spiess
Tristan Stuck
Vincenzo Festa
Franco Rolfo
Daniele Castelli
item.description: Abstract Deciphering the former presence of melt in metacarbonate rocks that experienced high dT/dP gradients is one of the most complex and debated topics in petrology. This is due to the high complexity in identifying and interpreting microstructures indicative of partial melting, as well as to the scarcity of experimental datasets in more complex carbonate-bearing compositional systems. Microstructures that are unequivocally interpreted as evidence of partial melting or melt–residue interaction in other lithologies have often been subject of conflicting interpretation when observed in metacarbonate rocks. From a literature review, it is clear that most microstructures suffering conflicting interpretation are preferentially found in granulite-facies olivine ± spinel-bearing calcite–dolomite and dolomite marbles. Here we present a collection of case studies that either attribute a suprasolidus or a subsolidus evolution of impure marbles sharing apparently indistinguishable microstructures, represented by symplectitic coronas around olivine enclosed in dolomite pockets. We have compared unambiguous anatectic microstructures with the experimental literature, which allowed us to discriminate whether dolomite or calcite melt pockets develop, according to the composition of the protolith. Additionally, through phase equilibrium thermodynamic modelling, we have tested the possibility that the symplectitic coronas around olivine, separated from calcite by the dolomite pockets, develop under subsolidus conditions. We show that this approach fails, and we demonstrate the reason by combining our results with the experimental background in the simplified CaO-MgO(-SiO2)-H2O-CO2 compositional space. Using this integrated approach, we speculate that olivine is a peritectic mineral that focuses the carbonatitic melt around itself, and that the crystallisation of the residual melt provides the H2O and CO2 necessary to stabilise amphibole and dolomite during cooling. Published: 2026-04-06T11:21:19+00:00 Venue: Journal of Petrology Score: 0.582 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Andrea Maroni; Fabrizio Tursi; Chiara Groppo; Eleanor C R Green; Richard Spiess; Tristan Stuck; Vincenzo Festa; Franco Rolfo; Daniele Castelli

Journal: Journal of Petrology

DOI: 10.1093/petrology/egag033

Relevance: 0.582 (consider)

Abstract Deciphering the former presence of melt in metacarbonate rocks that experienced high dT/dP gradients is one of the most complex and debated topics in petrology. This is due to the high complexity in identifying and interpreting microstructures indicative of partial melting, as well as to the scarcity of experimental datasets in more complex carbonate-bearing compositional systems. Microstructures that are unequivocally interpreted as evidence of partial melting or melt–residue interaction in other lithologies have often been subject of conflicting interpretation when observed in metacarbonate rocks. From a literature review, it is clear that most microstructures suffering conflicting interpretation are preferentially found in granulite-facies olivine ± spinel-bearing calcite–dolomite and dolomite marbles. Here we present a collection of case studies that either attribute a suprasolidus or a subsolidus evolution of impure marbles sharing apparently indistinguishable microstructures, represented by symplectitic coronas around olivine enclosed in dolomite pockets. We have compared unambiguous anatectic microstructures with the experimental literature, which allowed us to discriminate whether dolomite or calcite melt pockets develop, according to the composition of the protolith. Additionally, through phase equilibrium thermodynamic modelling, we have tested the possibility that the symplectitic coronas around olivine, separated from calcite by the dolomite pockets, develop under subsolidus conditions. We show that this approach fails, and we demonstrate the reason by combining our results with the experimental background in the simplified CaO-MgO(-SiO2)-H2O-CO2 compositional space. Using this integrated approach, we speculate that olivine is a peritectic mineral that focuses the carbonatitic melt around itself, and that the crystallisation of the residual melt provides the H2O and CO2 necessary to stabilise amphibole and dolomite during cooling.

item.title: Iron and zinc isotopic insights into the formation of Archean continental crust
item.dc:creator: Jianfeng Ma
Hamed Gamaleldien
Robert H. Smithies
Tim Johnson
Yuan-Ru Qu
Sheng-Ao Liu
Christopher L. Kirkland
item.description: Tonalite–trondhjemite–granodiorite (TTG) rocks that dominate Archean continental crust are traditionally viewed as representing the crystallized products of partial melting of hydrated basaltic rocks at various depths. However, the specific mechanisms and conditions of anatexis are debated, hindering our understanding of the tectonic mode of the early Earth. Here, we combine the Fe and Zn isotopic composition of TTG samples ( n = 9 ) from Western Australia with thermodynamic modelling to investigate their petrogenesis. The TTG have crystallization ages between 3.4 and 2.6 Gyr and compositions that range from primitive (K 2 O/Na 2 O 2 O/Na 2 O = 0.35–0.6, heavy zircon O isotopes). The samples have relatively homogeneous iron (δ 56 Fe = 0.13 ± 0.05‰ to 0.17 ± 0.05‰, all uncertainties at 2SD) and zinc (δ 66 Zn = 0.29 ± 0.04‰ to 0.35 ± 0.03‰) isotopic compositions. Thermodynamic modelling indicates that the formation of primitive and evolved TTGs was driven by partial melting of fully hydrated (H 2 O-saturated) but variably enriched basalts at temperatures ( T ) of 730–850 °C and pressures ( P ) of 2–15 kbar. Regional variations in T, P , and H 2 O content can account for the Fe–Zn isotope fractionation in the studied TTGs. The availability of sufficient H 2 O, potentially sourced from both mantle and crustal reservoirs, together with heat supplied by mafic underplating at depth or by mantle plumes or meteorite impacts at shallower crustal levels, could have facilitated TTG formation. Our results indicate that such processes are sufficient to explain the Fe–Zn isotope systematics without uniquely invoking subduction-driven accretionary or collisional orogenesis. Published: 2026-04-07T15:13:42+00:00 Venue: Earth and Planetary Science Letters Score: 0.582 (consider)

Authors: Jianfeng Ma; Hamed Gamaleldien; Robert H. Smithies; Tim Johnson; Yuan-Ru Qu; Sheng-Ao Liu; Christopher L. Kirkland

Journal: Earth and Planetary Science Letters

DOI: 10.1016/j.epsl.2026.120034

Relevance: 0.582 (consider)

Tonalite–trondhjemite–granodiorite (TTG) rocks that dominate Archean continental crust are traditionally viewed as representing the crystallized products of partial melting of hydrated basaltic rocks at various depths. However, the specific mechanisms and conditions of anatexis are debated, hindering our understanding of the tectonic mode of the early Earth. Here, we combine the Fe and Zn isotopic composition of TTG samples ( n = 9 ) from Western Australia with thermodynamic modelling to investigate their petrogenesis. The TTG have crystallization ages between 3.4 and 2.6 Gyr and compositions that range from primitive (K 2 O/Na 2 O 2 O/Na 2 O = 0.35–0.6, heavy zircon O isotopes). The samples have relatively homogeneous iron (δ 56 Fe = 0.13 ± 0.05‰ to 0.17 ± 0.05‰, all uncertainties at 2SD) and zinc (δ 66 Zn = 0.29 ± 0.04‰ to 0.35 ± 0.03‰) isotopic compositions. Thermodynamic modelling indicates that the formation of primitive and evolved TTGs was driven by partial melting of fully hydrated (H 2 O-saturated) but variably enriched basalts at temperatures ( T ) of 730–850 °C and pressures ( P ) of 2–15 kbar. Regional variations in T, P , and H 2 O content can account for the Fe–Zn isotope fractionation in the studied TTGs. The availability of sufficient H 2 O, potentially sourced from both mantle and crustal reservoirs, together with heat supplied by mafic underplating at depth or by mantle plumes or meteorite impacts at shallower crustal levels, could have facilitated TTG formation. Our results indicate that such processes are sufficient to explain the Fe–Zn isotope systematics without uniquely invoking subduction-driven accretionary or collisional orogenesis.

item.title: Unraveling superimposed mineralization of the Changce W–Pb–Zn–Ag deposit, Nanling Range, South China: Constraints from in-situ wolframite geochemistry and geochronology
item.dc:creator: Wenjie Fang
Yong-Jun Shao
Yi-Qu Xiong
Wei Liu
Xufeng Tian
Hongfei Di
Yingchun Rao
Kangqi Xu
item.description: The Changce deposit, located west of the world-class Yaogangxian W deposit in the central Nanling Range, represents a newly discovered metallogenic system with substantial potential for W–Pb–Zn–Ag mineralization and may be genetically related to regional W mineralization. However, the mineralization age and ore-forming processes remain poorly constrained, hampering further exploration. Integrating cross-cutting relationships, microtextural observations, BSE imaging, and multi-elemental mapping of wolframite, we divide the W mineralization into two metallogenic phases: Wol I (early phase) and Wol II (late phase). This study reports the first recognition and geochronological constraint of two distinct W mineralization events, namely Late Jurassic (160.9 ± 2.1 Ma, 2σ; MSWD = 0.98) and Early Cretaceous (144.2 ± 1.8 Ma, 2σ; MSWD = 0.97). Geochemical signatures show that both phases exhibit HREE enrichment and positive Eu anomalies, though Wol I displays significantly higher Nb, Ta, and REE contents, with a less pronounced Eu anomaly. These differences suggest that Wol I formed in a lower oxygen fugacity condition, with ore-forming fluids dominated by magmatic fluid mixed with meteoric water, while Wol II formed under relatively higher oxygen fugacity conditions, characterized by increased meteoric water participation. The divergent (Y + REE) / (Nb + Ta) ratios between Wol I and Wol II may further imply distinct fluid sources: Wol I precipitation might be genetically linked to Late Jurassic granite, whereas Wol II precipitation likely formed in association with Early Cretaceous granite. This study first establishes precise geochronological evidence for the Changce W–polymetallic deposit via wolframite U–Pb dating, confirming superimposed late Jurassic and early Cretaceous W mineralization in the Nanling Range. These findings provide essential evidence for the superimposed mineralization events in the Nanling Range, establishing a new model for deep exploration of the deposit and presenting important implications for regional exploration. Published: 2026-04-06T06:13:21+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geochemical Exploration Score: 0.580 (consider)

Journal: Journal of Geochemical Exploration

DOI: 10.1016/j.gexplo.2026.108059

Relevance: 0.580 (consider)

The Changce deposit, located west of the world-class Yaogangxian W deposit in the central Nanling Range, represents a newly discovered metallogenic system with substantial potential for W–Pb–Zn–Ag mineralization and may be genetically related to regional W mineralization. However, the mineralization age and ore-forming processes remain poorly constrained, hampering further exploration. Integrating cross-cutting relationships, microtextural observations, BSE imaging, and multi-elemental mapping of wolframite, we divide the W mineralization into two metallogenic phases: Wol I (early phase) and Wol II (late phase). This study reports the first recognition and geochronological constraint of two distinct W mineralization events, namely Late Jurassic (160.9 ± 2.1 Ma, 2σ; MSWD = 0.98) and Early Cretaceous (144.2 ± 1.8 Ma, 2σ; MSWD = 0.97). Geochemical signatures show that both phases exhibit HREE enrichment and positive Eu anomalies, though Wol I displays significantly higher Nb, Ta, and REE contents, with a less pronounced Eu anomaly. These differences suggest that Wol I formed in a lower oxygen fugacity condition, with ore-forming fluids dominated by magmatic fluid mixed with meteoric water, while Wol II formed under relatively higher oxygen fugacity conditions, characterized by increased meteoric water participation. The divergent (Y + REE) / (Nb + Ta) ratios between Wol I and Wol II may further imply distinct fluid sources: Wol I precipitation might be genetically linked to Late Jurassic granite, whereas Wol II precipitation likely formed in association with Early Cretaceous granite. This study first establishes precise geochronological evidence for the Changce W–polymetallic deposit via wolframite U–Pb dating, confirming superimposed late Jurassic and early Cretaceous W mineralization in the Nanling Range. These findings provide essential evidence for the superimposed mineralization events in the Nanling Range, establishing a new model for deep exploration of the deposit and presenting important implications for regional exploration.

item.title: Petrogenesis and metallogenic potential of the Dahenan complex in the Northern Taihang Mountains: Constraints from zircon genetic mineralogy and geochemistry
item.dc:creator: Lemin Zhang
Juquan Zhang
Xian Liang
Yibing Zhu
Chao Li
Long Zhang
Xiaohe Wu
item.description: The Dahenan complex is located within the northern sector of the Taihang Mountains Mesozoic metallogenic belt on the North China Craton (NCC). To constrain its magmatic evolution and metallogenic potential, we integrate whole-rock major and trace element geochemistry, zircon U Pb geochronology, zircon Lu Hf isotopes, and zircon morphology and typology for representative granitoids from the complex. Zircon U Pb ages indicate that the Dahenan granitoids were emplaced from the Late Jurassic to the Early Cretaceous (146.7 ± 2.5 to 130.5 ± 1.8 Ma), broadly coeval with craton reactivation, and comprises three distinct magmatic pulses (early ~147 Ma, middle 139–134 Ma, late 133–130 Ma). The granitoids exhibit high differentiation indices (DI = 73.17–97.03) and low solidification indices (SI = 1.18–13.46), collectively indicating a high degree of magmatic differentiation. Zircon ε Hf (t) values (−21.5 to −16.2) and two-stage Hf model ages (T DM2 = 2218–2545 Ma) plot between the 2.5 Ga crustal evolution array and an enriched-mantle endmember, indicating contributions from both crustal and mantle sources. Overall, Early Cretaceous slab rollback promoted magma mixing between melts derived from the partially melted enriched lithospheric mantle and the lower crust, followed by three episodes of ascent and emplacement. During the early stage (~147 Ma), granodiorites formed through differentiation in a deep-seated reservoir. During the middle stage (139–134 Ma), syenogranites were produced after the establishment of a shallow reservoir characterized by intensive alkali-feldspar crystallization. During the late stage (133–130 Ma), residual or newly injected hybrid magma differentiated at depth, then ascended rapidly and cooled to form monzogranites and porphyritic monzogranites. Furthermore, high oxygen fugacity (zircon, ΔFMQ ≈ 0.33–3.94), volatile enrichment, and evidence for fluid exsolution and evolution toward fluid saturation, together with a complex multi-level magmatic plumbing system that repeatedly penetrated the crust and mantle, indicate favorable conditions for the mobilization and enrichment of ore-forming elements such as Cu, Au, and Mo in this region. Published: 2026-04-06T06:13:21+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geochemical Exploration Score: 0.579 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lemin Zhang; Juquan Zhang; Xian Liang; Yibing Zhu; Chao Li; Long Zhang; Xiaohe Wu

Journal: Journal of Geochemical Exploration

DOI: 10.1016/j.gexplo.2026.108063

Relevance: 0.579 (consider)

The Dahenan complex is located within the northern sector of the Taihang Mountains Mesozoic metallogenic belt on the North China Craton (NCC). To constrain its magmatic evolution and metallogenic potential, we integrate whole-rock major and trace element geochemistry, zircon U Pb geochronology, zircon Lu Hf isotopes, and zircon morphology and typology for representative granitoids from the complex. Zircon U Pb ages indicate that the Dahenan granitoids were emplaced from the Late Jurassic to the Early Cretaceous (146.7 ± 2.5 to 130.5 ± 1.8 Ma), broadly coeval with craton reactivation, and comprises three distinct magmatic pulses (early ~147 Ma, middle 139–134 Ma, late 133–130 Ma). The granitoids exhibit high differentiation indices (DI = 73.17–97.03) and low solidification indices (SI = 1.18–13.46), collectively indicating a high degree of magmatic differentiation. Zircon ε Hf (t) values (−21.5 to −16.2) and two-stage Hf model ages (T DM2 = 2218–2545 Ma) plot between the 2.5 Ga crustal evolution array and an enriched-mantle endmember, indicating contributions from both crustal and mantle sources. Overall, Early Cretaceous slab rollback promoted magma mixing between melts derived from the partially melted enriched lithospheric mantle and the lower crust, followed by three episodes of ascent and emplacement. During the early stage (~147 Ma), granodiorites formed through differentiation in a deep-seated reservoir. During the middle stage (139–134 Ma), syenogranites were produced after the establishment of a shallow reservoir characterized by intensive alkali-feldspar crystallization. During the late stage (133–130 Ma), residual or newly injected hybrid magma differentiated at depth, then ascended rapidly and cooled to form monzogranites and porphyritic monzogranites. Furthermore, high oxygen fugacity (zircon, ΔFMQ ≈ 0.33–3.94), volatile enrichment, and evidence for fluid exsolution and evolution toward fluid saturation, together with a complex multi-level magmatic plumbing system that repeatedly penetrated the crust and mantle, indicate favorable conditions for the mobilization and enrichment of ore-forming elements such as Cu, Au, and Mo in this region.

item.title: Constraining the Relative Chronology of Repeated Large Earthquakes in the Longmen Shan Thrust Belt, Eastern Tibetan Plateau
item.dc:creator: Lei Zhang
Haibing Li
Zhiming Sun
Yong Cao
Chunrui Li
Huan Wang
Yong Zheng
Jialiang Si
item.description: Abstract The Yingxiu–Beichuan Fault Zone (YBFZ), located in the Longmen Shan Thrust Belt (LSTB) on the eastern Tibetan Plateau, is highly seismically active. However, direct evidence of large earthquakes at depth is scarce. Here, we present rock magnetic, microstructural, and geochemical analyses of four fault zones from the Wenchuan Earthquake Fault Scientific Drilling borehole 2. Results show that fault gouges have high magnetic susceptibility, contain neoformed magnetite and monoclinic pyrrhotite, providing direct evidence for repeated large earthquakes with frictional heating temperatures of ∼500–900°C at depth. These earthquakes occurred in a reducing, sulfide‐bearing fluid environment throughout the seismic cycle. The increasing abundance of neoformed ferromagnetic minerals with depth could be related to more recent large earthquakes propagating at greater depths, supporting a structural model of vertical stacking and eastward propagation of the LSTB. This study provides key constraints on the deep seismic history and tectonic evolution of the LSTB. Published: 2026-04-08T14:31:36+00:00 Venue: Geophysical Research Letters Score: 0.579 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Lei Zhang; Haibing Li; Zhiming Sun; Yong Cao; Chunrui Li; Huan Wang; Yong Zheng; Jialiang Si

Journal: Geophysical Research Letters

DOI: 10.1029/2025gl121166

Relevance: 0.579 (consider)

Abstract The Yingxiu–Beichuan Fault Zone (YBFZ), located in the Longmen Shan Thrust Belt (LSTB) on the eastern Tibetan Plateau, is highly seismically active. However, direct evidence of large earthquakes at depth is scarce. Here, we present rock magnetic, microstructural, and geochemical analyses of four fault zones from the Wenchuan Earthquake Fault Scientific Drilling borehole 2. Results show that fault gouges have high magnetic susceptibility, contain neoformed magnetite and monoclinic pyrrhotite, providing direct evidence for repeated large earthquakes with frictional heating temperatures of ∼500–900°C at depth. These earthquakes occurred in a reducing, sulfide‐bearing fluid environment throughout the seismic cycle. The increasing abundance of neoformed ferromagnetic minerals with depth could be related to more recent large earthquakes propagating at greater depths, supporting a structural model of vertical stacking and eastward propagation of the LSTB. This study provides key constraints on the deep seismic history and tectonic evolution of the LSTB.

item.title: Fe–Mn Crust δ 98 Mo Decline Records Tectonic Forcing on Marine Oxide Sink Reduction During the Early Eocene–Early Oligocene
item.dc:creator: Limin Zhang
Jiangbo Ren
Hongjin Chen
Haozhe Wang
Zhen Sun
Yong Yang
Huodai Zhang
Zhenquan Wei
Lixue Zhang
Yingzhi Ren
Gaowen He
item.description: Abstract S, Tl, and W isotope records document pronounced early Eocene shifts in marine redox, yet the absence of a Mo isotope record obscures its progression. Here, we present ∼80 Myr Mo–Os–Be–Nd isotope profiles from a Northwest Pacific hydrogenetic Fe–Mn crust, revealing a systematic δ 98 Mo decline from −0.24‰ (early Eocene) to −0.83‰ (early Oligocene). This trend challenges the notion of Cenozoic Mo isotope stability and mainly reflects a reduced isotopic fractionation during marine Mo removal (ΔSW‐sink) from a contracted oxic sink. Coupled Mo–S–Tl–W isotope records suggest that this contraction was primarily driven by the tectonically forced loss of extensive shelf environments during the initial India–Asia collision. We establish Fe–Mn crust δ 98 Mo as a reliable proxy for global seawater redox evolution, demonstrating that multi‐proxy integration resolves tectonic controls on Earth's long‐term oxygenation history. Published: 2026-04-06T03:35:07+00:00 Venue: Geophysical Research Letters Score: 0.578 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Limin Zhang; Jiangbo Ren; Hongjin Chen; Haozhe Wang; Zhen Sun; Yong Yang; Huodai Zhang; Zhenquan Wei; Lixue Zhang; Yingzhi Ren; Gaowen He

Journal: Geophysical Research Letters

DOI: 10.1029/2025gl120782

Relevance: 0.578 (consider)

Abstract S, Tl, and W isotope records document pronounced early Eocene shifts in marine redox, yet the absence of a Mo isotope record obscures its progression. Here, we present ∼80 Myr Mo–Os–Be–Nd isotope profiles from a Northwest Pacific hydrogenetic Fe–Mn crust, revealing a systematic δ 98 Mo decline from −0.24‰ (early Eocene) to −0.83‰ (early Oligocene). This trend challenges the notion of Cenozoic Mo isotope stability and mainly reflects a reduced isotopic fractionation during marine Mo removal (ΔSW‐sink) from a contracted oxic sink. Coupled Mo–S–Tl–W isotope records suggest that this contraction was primarily driven by the tectonically forced loss of extensive shelf environments during the initial India–Asia collision. We establish Fe–Mn crust δ 98 Mo as a reliable proxy for global seawater redox evolution, demonstrating that multi‐proxy integration resolves tectonic controls on Earth's long‐term oxygenation history.

item.title: Synchronous Re-Os and Rb-Sr isotopic disturbance by the Emeishan large igneous province: Insights from hydrothermally recrystallized chalcopyrite in the Jianglang Dome, eastern Tibetan Plateau
item.dc:creator: Yanpei Dai
Yudi Zhu
Tongzhu Li
Huihua Zhang
item.description: Chalcopyrite Re-Os and Rb-Sr chronometers during high-temperature magmatism remain poorly examined. This paper reports geochemical and zircon U-Pb dating results for pillow basalts, as well as Re-Os, Rb-Sr and He-Ar isotopic data for hydrothermally recrystallized chalcopyrite in the Jianglang Dome, eastern Tibetan Plateau. The pillow basalts were formed at 257.4 Ma ( n = 15, MSWD = 0.63), with Ti-in-zircon temperatures of 757–952 °C. This suggests an affinity with ca. 260 Ma Emeishan large igneous province. Recrystallized chalcopyrite grains were all separated from massive ores in the stratiform Cu deposits. They define a Re-Os isochron age of 257 Ma ( n = 5, MSWD = 5.9), and a Rb-Sr isochron age of 258.1 Ma ( n = 4, MSWD = 2.6). These consistent age data are attributed to either metal derivation from the pillow basalts, or partially isotopic disturbance by high-temperature mafic magmatism. However, chalcopyrite samples yield continental crust-like high initial 187 Os/ 188 Os ratio of 1.006, high initial 87 Sr/ 86 Sr ratio of 0.71426 and low 3 He/ 4 He ratios of 0.072–0.187 Ra ( n = 8). This indicates a dominant crustal origin for metals and ore-forming fluids, containing only 1.02–2.91% mantle input. The Late Permian high-temperature pillow basalts with obvious mantle signatures, were thus not responsible for epigenetic Cu mineralization. Alternatively, they incompletely disturbed chalcopyrite Re-Os and Rb-Sr isotope systematics with low closure temperatures (300–400 °C), yielding isochron ages without metallogenic significance. Our new findings confirm that integrated mineralization age determinations are necessary where magmatism occurred. Published: 2026-04-06T06:13:18+00:00 Venue: Journal of Geochemical Exploration Score: 0.576 (consider)
item.content:encoded:
Authors: Yanpei Dai; Yudi Zhu; Tongzhu Li; Huihua Zhang

Journal: Journal of Geochemical Exploration

DOI: 10.1016/j.gexplo.2026.108067

Relevance: 0.576 (consider)

Chalcopyrite Re-Os and Rb-Sr chronometers during high-temperature magmatism remain poorly examined. This paper reports geochemical and zircon U-Pb dating results for pillow basalts, as well as Re-Os, Rb-Sr and He-Ar isotopic data for hydrothermally recrystallized chalcopyrite in the Jianglang Dome, eastern Tibetan Plateau. The pillow basalts were formed at 257.4 Ma ( n = 15, MSWD = 0.63), with Ti-in-zircon temperatures of 757–952 °C. This suggests an affinity with ca. 260 Ma Emeishan large igneous province. Recrystallized chalcopyrite grains were all separated from massive ores in the stratiform Cu deposits. They define a Re-Os isochron age of 257 Ma ( n = 5, MSWD = 5.9), and a Rb-Sr isochron age of 258.1 Ma ( n = 4, MSWD = 2.6). These consistent age data are attributed to either metal derivation from the pillow basalts, or partially isotopic disturbance by high-temperature mafic magmatism. However, chalcopyrite samples yield continental crust-like high initial 187 Os/ 188 Os ratio of 1.006, high initial 87 Sr/ 86 Sr ratio of 0.71426 and low 3 He/ 4 He ratios of 0.072–0.187 Ra ( n = 8). This indicates a dominant crustal origin for metals and ore-forming fluids, containing only 1.02–2.91% mantle input. The Late Permian high-temperature pillow basalts with obvious mantle signatures, were thus not responsible for epigenetic Cu mineralization. Alternatively, they incompletely disturbed chalcopyrite Re-Os and Rb-Sr isotope systematics with low closure temperatures (300–400 °C), yielding isochron ages without metallogenic significance. Our new findings confirm that integrated mineralization age determinations are necessary where magmatism occurred.
